content:
Testing agents is no longer enough. Modern AI systems are shifting toward continuous execution loops, self-correction, and iterative reasoning to solve real-world problems.

4 min read
Just now

--

Ninety one percent of my agent’s test cases passed. The agent still shipped a bug that took down a real workflow in its first week of production traffic. That gap between test pass rate and real world failure is the entire reason harnesses are losing their grip on how serious teams evaluate agents.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

For a long time, a harness felt like proof. Write forty prompts, check forty outputs, watch the green checkmarks pile up, ship it. That process works when the input space is small and closed. It falls apart the moment an agent has to act inside a world that talks back: a tool that returns a partial result, a document that contradicts another document, a step three that depends on a guess made in step one. None of that shows up in a fixed test suite, because a fixed test suite only knows the questions you thought to ask it.
The Test Suite Was Measuring The Wrong Thing
Here is the uncomfortable part. A harness does not test whether an agent is good. It tests whether an agent…
